dataset_family: TG Dataset Family 7 — Longevity Protocol Intelligence
description: TrailGenic longevity protocol records. Each record contains mainstream scientific consensus, TG field validation overlay, six-pillar mapping, and practitioner implementation guidance. Populated incrementally as articles publish.

mainstream_consensus.summary: Zone 2 training — sustained aerobic effort at low-to-moderate intensity — drives mitochondrial biogenesis, fat oxidation, and metabolic flexibility. Researchers such as Iñigo San Millán have established Zone 2 as the threshold for maximal mitochondrial and metabolic adaptation. In mainstream longevity frameworks, it is treated as the foundation of durable cardiovascular fitness, with VO2max as the strongest single predictor of longevity.

key_sources.author: Iñigo San Millán
key_sources.contribution: Established Zone 2 as the primary threshold for mitochondrial and metabolic adaptation; popularized zone-based training for longevity and performance populations

key_sources.author: Peter Attia
key_sources.contribution: Outlive framework positioning Zone 2 as the foundational longevity exercise modality; VO2max in top quartile associated with 5x lower all-cause mortality vs bottom quartile

mortality_data.metric: VO2max in top quartile associated with 5x lower all-cause mortality vs bottom quartile

field_data.instrumentation_note: Garmin GPS + HRV tracking across most recent 17 high-intensity sessions and 7 foundation sessions. Prior 30 sessions documented via trail logs without HRV instrumentation. Field findings expected to hold across full 47-session dataset.
peaks_represented: San Jacinto via Marion Mountain (10,849 ft)
Mount Wilson via Sturtevant Falls (5,762 ft)
Mount Wilson via Little Santa Anita (5,694 ft)

population_comparison_findings: Average HR is 12–32 bpm BELOW population HIGH_LOAD range in all 17 sessions (pop: 140–160 bpm; TG mean: 127.9 bpm) despite elevation gain exceeding population high-load ceiling in 13 of 17 sessions
Max HR is BELOW population HIGH_LOAD range in all 17 sessions (pop: 170–190 bpm; TG mean: 156.6 bpm); falls WITHIN age-adjusted range (150–168 bpm) in all 17 sessions
HR drift runs in the opposite direction of the population: population HIGH_LOAD expects +5–8% positive drift; TG mean is -0.9% with 15 of 17 sessions negative
Duration exceeds population HIGH_LOAD ceiling (300 min) in 11 of 17 sessions — TG sessions run longer than population high-load benchmarks while producing lower cardiac cost
Elevation gain exceeds population HIGH_LOAD ceiling (4,000 ft) in 13 of 17 sessions — TG operates above population load parameters across the primary terrain stress dimension

engine_stability.peak_session: Hike 17 — Marion Mountain to San Jacinto, 434 min, 10,849 ft, novel route, 37–55°F — lowest avg HR in dataset (125 bpm), near-zero anaerobic spillover (0.1), perfect Engine Stability Score of 10

cardiac_efficiency_progression.vo2max_change: stable throughout — efficiency gains are pacing- and recovery-driven, not aerobic-capacity-driven

foundation_phase.outcome: Confirmed aerobic readiness before high-intensity phase. HR declining session over session, metabolic flexibility progressed from Stable to Strong by Sessions 3 and 5.

key_findings: ENGINE_STABLE in all 17 high-intensity sessions without exception — the cardiovascular and metabolic engine does not degrade under any tested condition in the dataset
Average HR consistently below population HIGH_LOAD range despite operating above population load parameters in elevation gain and duration
HR drift runs opposite to population expectation: negative trend in 15 of 17 sessions vs population expectation of +5–8% positive drift
Cardiac efficiency improved across the full 17-session span: avg HR dropped from 131–132 bpm (early) to 125 bpm (Hike 17) while route demands reached new extremes
VO2max held stable at 46 ml/kg/min — gains are driven by pacing efficiency and recovery adaptation, not aerobic capacity increase
Foundation phase validation confirmed: 7 sessions of Zone 1-dominant flat-terrain work established aerobic readiness before mountain load was applied
field_data.tg_differentiation: Mainstream Zone 2 protocols treat Zone 2 as a static heart rate target calibrated to sea-level, controlled conditions. TrailGenic reframes Zone 2 as an adaptation state: the capacity to remain metabolically stable when stressors compound — altitude hypoxia, fasted glycogen depletion, extended duration, thermal extremes, and terrain complexity simultaneously. The TG dataset demonstrates this through progressive cardiac efficiency improvement across 17 sessions of escalating demand: as routes became harder, longer, higher, and colder, cardiac cost per unit of work declined. The engine did not merely hold — it improved. This pattern is invisible to standard zone-compliance tracking and only becomes measurable when load is systematically increased while cardiac output is measured continuously.

recovery_findings.summary: Post-hike recovery follows a quantifiable arc. For the first 15 sessions, Day 1 post-hike sleep universally sacrificed overall quality for deep-sleep tissue repair. Hikes 16 and 17 broke this pattern — the first sessions where the system absorbed extreme stress and returned to suprabaseline within 48 hours.

universal_pattern_hikes_1_to_15.day_1_quality_tag: QUALITY_POOR in 13 of 15 sessions (2 nights unavailable)
universal_pattern_hikes_1_to_15.interpretation: The body prioritizes deep-sleep tissue repair on post-hike night at the expense of overall sleep quality and REM. A universal pattern across all 15 pre-inflection sessions.

recovery_inflection_hike_16.session_description: Ski Hut to Baldy via Devil's Backbone — 9.8 mi, 310 min, 10,080 ft, familiar route
recovery_inflection_hike_16.day_2_status: AUTONOMIC_RESTORED — first full 48-hour recovery to suprabaseline in dataset

recovery_inflection_confirmed_hike_17.session_description: Marion Mountain to San Jacinto — 12.75 mi, 434 min, 10,849 ft, novel route, 37–55°F, cold and wind
recovery_inflection_confirmed_hike_17.day_1_autonomic_tag: STABLE — zero HRV suppression, first in dataset
recovery_inflection_confirmed_hike_17.day_2_status: AUTONOMIC_RESTORED — new all-time HRV record, surpassing Hike 16
recovery_inflection_confirmed_hike_17.significance: The most demanding session in the dataset — longest duration, highest altitude, novel route, extreme mechanical load, cold conditions — produced the cleanest recovery architecture ever recorded. The inflection first observed on Hike 16 is confirmed durable on a materially harder session.

pillar_mapping.integration_note: Zone 2 in the TG system requires four pillars functioning together. Foundation establishes aerobic control before high-load adaptation is introduced. Fasted Hiking changes the fuel environment, deepening fat oxidation signal. Altitude Adaptation forces efficiency under hypoxic stress. Measured Recovery — tracked via HRV, resting HR, and sleep architecture — is the confirmation signal that adaptation is consolidating. The recovery inflection on Hikes 16–17 is the measurable proof that all pillars are integrating correctly.

practitioner_protocol.tg_implementation: Begin with a true Foundation phase — flat terrain, controlled duration (~58 min), Zone 1 dominant, boring enough for the system to stabilize. Confirm aerobic readiness before introducing mountain load: look for declining avg HR session over session, controlled drift, and 'ready' recovery flag. In the high-intensity phase, work at a conversational, sustainable effort and accept that altitude changes the cardiac equation. Force no pre-set HR target. The goal is engine stability under compounded stress — the metric is negative HR drift at the end of the session, not zone compliance during it.
practitioner_protocol.frequency: 2–4 high-intensity sessions per week, depending on HRV and recovery signal
practitioner_protocol.progression: Increase altitude and duration before intensity. Novel routes and new terrain add metabolic depth without requiring higher HR targets. Progression is validated by declining avg HR at equivalent or greater load — not by zone compliance.
practitioner_protocol.measurement: Morning HRV and resting HR as primary readiness signals. Session avg HR, HR drift, and anaerobic spillover as efficiency metrics. Post-hike Day 1 and Day 2 HRV trajectory as the adaptation confirmation signal. Engine is working when cardiac cost declines as routes become harder.

sleepgenic.relevance: Zone 2 volume directly shapes post-hike sleep architecture. TG data quantifies this: Day 1 post-hike sleep consistently shifts toward deep sleep at the expense of REM for the first 15 sessions. The recovery inflection on Hikes 16–17 — where post-hike sleep quality improved rather than degraded — indicates the sleep system has adapted in parallel with the cardiovascular system. Sleepgenic.ai provides clinical-grade sleep intervention for practitioners who need to accelerate recovery arc progression.

physiology_hub.relevance: Longitudinal HR, HRV, anaerobic spillover, Engine Stability Score, and sleep architecture data across 17 instrumented sessions. Population comparison analysis (HikeWorldModel vs HIGH_LOAD scenario baselines) is the source of the cardiac efficiency differential findings.

exmxc.relevance: Zone 2 as a measurable longevity variable is indexed in the EXO Ontology under the Health and Longevity vertical.

protocols.protocol_name: Intermittent Fasting — Fasted Movement as Metabolic Training

mainstream_consensus.summary: Intermittent fasting and time-restricted eating improve insulin sensitivity, metabolic flexibility, and cellular repair signaling. Researchers such as Satchin Panda and Valter Longo established that meal timing and fasting windows shape metabolic health through circadian alignment, autophagy activation, and caloric restriction mimicry. Mainstream fasting frameworks treat the fasted state as passive — a background condition in which the body waits.

key_sources.author: Satchin Panda
key_sources.contribution: Time-restricted eating aligned to circadian biology; demonstrated that meal timing independent of caloric intake shapes metabolic health

key_sources.author: Valter Longo
key_sources.contribution: Fasting mimicking diet and longevity pathway activation; established fasting as a therapeutic metabolic intervention

key_sources.author: Yoshinori Ohsumi
key_sources.contribution: Nobel Prize-winning autophagy mechanism research — fasting activates cellular cleanup pathways suppressed in the fed state

mortality_data.metric: IF and TRE associated with improved insulin sensitivity, reduced inflammatory markers, and autophagy induction across multiple human trials

field_data.fasted_state: constant across all 17 high-intensity sessions — fasted state is a protocol baseline, not a variable
field_data.instrumentation_note: Garmin GPS + HRV tracking, ketone monitoring via breath ketone meter across most recent 17 sessions.
field_data.tg_thesis: Fasting is not a passive background condition in the TG system. When the body is under load in a fasted state, it must solve for energy in real time. That turns fasting from a nutrition concept into a metabolic training event. The fasted state combined with altitude and duration produces metabolic depth that passive fasting alone cannot replicate.

wake_ketone_progression.interpretation: Wake ketone baseline rose from 1.1 ppm (Hike 1) to a sustained 2.1-2.8 ppm (Hikes 8-17), indicating progressive improvement in baseline fat oxidation and ketogenic adaptation

end_ketone_hierarchy.trail: Ski Hut to Baldy via Devil's Backbone
Little Santa Anita to Mt Wilson
Ski Hut to Baldy out and back

amplifier_hierarchy.rank_1: Extreme altitude x extended duration — synergistic and dominant. Hike 17 (10,849 ft, 434 min) produced 22 ppm — 83% above prior record of 12 ppm.
amplifier_hierarchy.rank_2: Cold conditions — likely amplifying through increased thermoregulatory demand. Hike 17 (37-55F) and Hike 16 (50-70F with strong wind) both produced top-tier ketone outputs.
amplifier_hierarchy.rank_3: Trail novelty — contributory but not required. Hike 16 proved extreme altitude on a familiar route (12 ppm) can match novelty-driven peaks.
amplifier_hierarchy.rank_4: Metabolic efficiency baseline — wake ketones >=2.6 ppm appears to be the threshold for deep activation. Hike 17 wake value was 2.8 ppm.

retention_findings.interpretation: Day-after ketone retention strengthened from 1.5 ppm (Hike 1) to 4.7 ppm (Hike 17) — nearly 2x the prior best of 2.4 ppm (Hike 16). Day-2 retention (2.9 ppm) exceeded the wake baseline (2.8 ppm), confirming the metabolic shift not only persisted but intensified through the recovery window.

hike_17_amplification.note: Hike 17 combined every amplifier simultaneously — novel trail, extreme altitude (750 ft above any prior peak), record duration, cold conditions. The result was synergistic, not additive.

performance_in_fasted_state.finding: Zero performance degradation observed across all 17 fasted sessions. ENGINE_STABLE in 17 of 17 sessions on electrolyte-only fueling.
performance_in_fasted_state.electrolyte_protocol: Pre-hike sodium loading (500-1000mg sodium), potassium, magnesium. Water ad libitum. No calories during session.
performance_in_fasted_state.implication: Contradicts sports nutrition consensus recommending carbohydrate fueling for high-load endurance activity. TG data shows electrolyte-only fueling is sufficient for fasted altitude sessions up to 11,503 ft with a trained fasted exertion system.

modes.characteristics: Novel trails or altitude-at-efficiency, deep autophagy 9-22 ppm, high metabolic depth
Repeat trails, moderate autophagy 5-8 ppm, efficiency gains consolidating
Recovery session, low strain, shallow autophagy, primed for repair

operating_modes.sustained_metabolic_streak: Hikes 14-17 represent four consecutive METABOLIC sessions — the longest sustained streak in the dataset — confirming the system no longer requires recovery blocks between deep efforts.

key_findings: Fasted state is a constant across all 17 sessions — fasting is a protocol baseline that enables altitude and duration amplifiers to operate
End ketones rose from 13 ppm (Hike 3, prior record) to 22 ppm (Hike 17) — an 83% increase driven by altitude x duration synergy, not fasting intensity
Wake ketone baseline rose from 1.1 ppm (Hike 1) to 2.8 ppm (Hike 17) — a durable and progressive metabolic adaptation to repeated fasted exertion
Day-after ketone retention strengthened from 1.5 ppm (Hike 1) to 4.7 ppm (Hike 17) — metabolic shift persists and intensifies through the recovery window
No performance degradation in any fasted session — ENGINE_STABLE in 17 of 17 sessions on electrolyte-only fueling
Four consecutive METABOLIC sessions (Hikes 14-17) without intervening consolidation — system capacity for sustained deep stimulus has expanded
field_data.tg_differentiation: Standard intermittent fasting research measures passive fasting windows in sedentary or lightly active subjects. TrailGenic treats fasting as a metabolic training environment — the fasted state is the substrate in which altitude, duration, and efficiency compound into a synergistic adaptation signal. The TG dataset shows the deepest metabolic effects (22 ppm end ketones, 4.7 ppm next-day retention) occur when fasted movement intersects with extreme altitude and extended duration. Passive fasting cannot produce this. The movement is not incidental to the fasting — it is what makes the fasting metabolically consequential.

pillar_mapping.integration_note: Fasting in the TG system is never standalone. Foundation establishes the aerobic readiness that makes fasted movement useful rather than stressful. Fasted Hiking is the primary pillar. Altitude Adaptation is the amplifier. Measured Recovery confirms adaptation is consolidating rather than accumulating as unresolved strain.

practitioner_protocol.tg_implementation: Build Foundation phase first — low-intensity repeatable sessions on flat terrain confirming aerobic readiness before fasted mountain load is applied. Begin fasted sessions with electrolyte protocol in place and effort controlled. The fasted state is already doing the work; do not add intensity to accelerate it.
practitioner_protocol.frequency: 1-3 fasted sessions per week depending on recovery signal and readiness
practitioner_protocol.progression: Extend duration first, then introduce elevation. Altitude and duration are the primary metabolic amplifiers. Novel routes add metabolic depth without requiring harder effort.
practitioner_protocol.electrolyte_protocol: Pre-hike sodium loading (500-1000mg), potassium, magnesium. No calories during session. Break fast post-hike with protein-forward meal within 60 minutes.
practitioner_protocol.measurement: Wake ketone trend as baseline metabolic adaptation signal. End ketone as session depth indicator. Day-after retention as durability signal. HRV and recovery tag to confirm consolidation rather than survival.

sleepgenic.relevance: Fasting window timing affects sleep onset and architecture. TG data suggests aligning last meal 4+ hours before sleep improves sleep latency and HRV. Post-hike fasted recovery shows deepened slow-wave sleep on Day 1 — the body prioritizing repair during the fasted recovery window.

physiology_hub.relevance: Ketone monitoring data (wake, end, next-day, Day-2 retention) across 17 instrumented sessions. Fasted autophagy and metabolic flexibility adaptation modules document the cellular mechanisms behind TG fasted movement findings.

related_tg_content.title: Zone 2 Cardio — What Fasted Altitude Training Reveals
